title: 10 Top-Rated Herbal Creams for Reducing Menstrual Cramps

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Gingerol is good for menstrual cramps because it helps to reduce inflammation and relax muscles, which can ease the pain and discomfort associated with menstrual cramps.
Shogaol is good for menstrual cramps because it has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce pain and swelling in the uterus, thereby providing relief from menstrual cramps.
Zingiberene is good for menstrual cramps because it has anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that help reduce pain and discomfort in the uterus during menstrual periods.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale cream, also known as ginger cream, is often used to help ease menstrual cramps. This cream contains a substance called gingerol, which has anti-inflammatory properties. When applied to the skin, gingerol helps to reduce swelling and pain in the abdomen. Menstrual cramps occur when the uterus contracts to release blood, and this contraction can be painful. Gingerol in the cream works to relax the uterus muscles, making the contractions less painful. Additionally, gingerol has a warming effect on the body, which can help to increase blood flow and reduce pain. Many women find that using a Zingiber officinale cream during their period helps to reduce the severity of their cramps and makes them feel more comfortable. The cream is also easy to apply and can be used in combination with other pain-relieving methods, such as heat or medication.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hypericum perforatum herbal cream for menstrual cramps, start by drying the St. John's Wort flowers and leaves. You can dry them in a low oven (150-200°F) for 1-2 hours or air dry them in a warm, dark place for several days. Once the herbs are dry, grind them into a fine powder using a coffee grinder or mortar and pestle. Next, mix 2-3 tablespoons of the herbal powder with 1/4 cup of carrier oil, such as coconut or olive oil, in a small bowl. Let the mixture sit for 2-3 weeks, shaking the bowl every few days, allowing the herbal properties to infuse into the oil. After the infusion period, strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another bowl, discarding the solids. Finally, add a small amount of beeswax and a preservative like vitamin E oil to the infused oil, and heat the mixture gently in a double boiler until the wax is melted and the mixture is smooth. Pour the cream into a container, let it cool, and it's ready to use as a natural remedy for menstrual cramps.
remedies.image_alt: Zingiber officinale herbal creams for menstrual cramps

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Actein is good for menstrual cramps because it has anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce pain and relax the muscles in the uterus, thereby providing relief from cramps and discomfort.
Isoferulic acid helps with menstrual cramps because it has anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that reduce pain and swelling in the uterus during menstruation.

Phenolic acids are good for menstrual cramps because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce pain and discomfort by blocking the production of prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause uterine contractions and lead to cramping.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cimicifuga racemosa creams are made from the roots of the black cohosh plant. For centuries, people have used black cohosh to help with menstrual cramps. This is because the plant contains chemicals that can help to relax the muscles in the uterus and reduce inflammation. When you have menstrual cramps, the muscles in your uterus contract and get tight, causing pain. Cimicifuga racemosa creams work by blocking the chemical signals that make these muscles contract. This helps to stop the pain and discomfort associated with menstrual cramps. The creams are applied directly to the skin, usually on the abdomen or lower back, where the pain is felt. They can also be absorbed into the bloodstream, where they can help to ease the pain from the inside out. Some people find that Cimicifuga racemosa creams are more effective when used in combination with other natural remedies, such as heat or relaxation techniques.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hypericum perforatum herbal creams that help with menstrual cramps, first you need to prepare the herbal extract. Start by drying 20-30 grams of fresh Hypericum perforatum leaves in a low-temperature oven at 50-60°C for 2-3 hours. Then, grind the dried leaves into a fine powder using a coffee grinder or mortar and pestle. Next, mix 10 grams of the powdered leaves with 100 grams of carrier oil such as coconut or sweet almond oil in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water. Let the mixture steep for 2-3 hours, stirring occasionally, to allow the herbal properties to infuse into the oil. After the infusion is complete, strain the oil through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean bowl, discarding the solids. Now, to make the cream, mix 100 grams of the infused oil with 100 grams of beeswax and 50 grams of emulsifying wax in a heat-proof bowl. Melt the mixture in a double boiler or in the microwave in 10-second increments, stirring between each heating. Once the mixture is fully melted, add 10 grams of vitamin E oil and 10 grams of essential oil (such as lavender or peppermint) if desired, and stir well. Pour the mixture into a clean container, allow it to cool and solidify, and then use it as needed to help relieve menstrual cramps.
remedies.image_alt: Cimicifuga racemosa herbal creams for menstrual cramps

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Glycyrrhizin is good for menstrual cramps because it helps to reduce inflammation and relax muscles, which can ease the pain and discomfort caused by cramps.
Flavonoids are good for menstrual cramps because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce pain and discomfort by blocking the release of certain chemicals that cause cramping.

Saponins are good for menstrual cramps because they have anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, which help to reduce pain and inflammation in the uterus, thereby providing relief from menstrual cramps.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra creams are good for menstrual cramps because they contain a powerful ingredient called glycyrrhizin. This ingredient has anti-inflammatory properties, which help reduce swelling and ease pain in the body. When a woman experiences menstrual cramps, her uterus contracts and gets inflamed, causing severe pain. Glycyrrhizin in the cream helps to reduce this inflammation and relax the uterine muscles, providing relief from cramps. Additionally, glycyrrhizin has a soothing effect on the body, helping to calm down the nervous system and reduce stress and anxiety associated with menstrual cramps. This, in turn, helps to reduce the intensity and frequency of menstrual cramps. The cream also moisturizes and soothes the skin, reducing any irritation or redness caused by the cramps.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hypericum perforatum herbal cream for menstrual cramps, you will need dried Hypericum flowers and a few simple ingredients. First, combine 2 tablespoons of dried Hypericum flowers with 2 cups of boiling water in a heat-resistant bowl. Let it steep for 10-15 minutes, then strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean bowl. Allow the mixture to cool down, then add 1 cup of coconut oil or other natural oil, 2 tablespoons of beeswax, and 1 teaspoon of vitamin E oil to the cooled liquid. Stir the mixture well and place it in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water. Heat the mixture gently, stirring occasionally, until the beeswax has melted and the mixture has thickened into a smooth cream. Remove the bowl from the heat and let the cream cool and thicken further before transferring it to a clean container. Once the cream has cooled and solidified, you can apply it directly to the skin to help soothe menstrual cramps.
remedies.image_alt: Glycyrrhiza glabra herbal creams for menstrual cramps

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Coumarins are good for menstrual cramps because they have anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving properties that help to reduce the severity and duration of menstrual cramps by inhibiting the production of prostaglandins, which cause pain and discomfort during menstruation.
Phenolic acids are good for menstrual cramps because they have anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce pain and swelling in the uterus and surrounding areas.

Butylphthalide, also known as St. John's Wort, is good for menstrual cramps because it has anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving properties that can help to reduce the severity and frequency of cramps.
Furocoumarins are good for menstrual cramps because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce pain and relax the muscles in the uterus.
Tannins, also known as St. John's Wort, are good for menstrual cramps because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce pain and swelling in the uterus.

remedies.remedy_desc: Angelica archangelica cream is a natural remedy that can help with menstrual cramps. The Angelica plant has been used for centuries to treat various health issues, including pain and inflammation. The cream is made from the root of the plant, which contains compounds like ferulic acid and volatile oils. These compounds have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce swelling and ease pain in the uterus. When applied topically, the cream can help relax the uterine muscles, reducing cramping and discomfort. It can also help to reduce prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause the uterus to contract and lead to pain. Additionally, the cream has antispasmodic properties, which can help calm muscle spasms and ease cramping. Many women have found relief from menstrual cramps by using Angelica archangelica cream, and it is often recommended as a natural alternative to pain relief medication. The cream can be applied directly to the lower abdomen, where it can be absorbed into the skin and start working to ease cramping and discomfort.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hypericum perforatum herbal cream for menstrual cramps, you'll need dried Hypericum flowers, coconut oil, beeswax, vitamin E oil, and a double boiler. First, measure out 1/4 cup of dried Hypericum flowers and place them in a heat-resistant bowl. Next, combine 1/2 cup of coconut oil and 2 tablespoons of beeswax in the double boiler and heat until the beeswax melts. Once the mixture is smooth, add 2 tablespoons of dried Hypericum flowers and let it steep for 2-3 hours. Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a separate container, and discard the solids. Finally, add 1 teaspoon of vitamin E oil and mix well. Pour the mixture into a glass jar and let it cool and solidify before use. You can apply this cream to the lower abdomen to help soothe menstrual cramps and ease discomfort.

remedies.remedy_desc: Valeriana officinalis, also known as valerian root, is a natural ingredient used in creams to help with menstrual cramps. The root of the valerian plant contains a chemical called valerenic acid, which has a calming effect on the body. When applied topically in a cream, it helps to relax the muscles in the uterus and other parts of the body, reducing cramping and discomfort. Valerian root has been used for centuries to treat various types of pain, including menstrual cramps. It is also known for its anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce swelling and ease pain. Additionally, valerian root has a sedative effect, which can help to promote relaxation and reduce stress, further alleviating menstrual cramp symptoms. When applied in a cream, valerian root is easily absorbed into the skin, providing quick relief from menstrual cramps.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hypericum perforatum herbal creams for menstrual cramps, start by drying the St. John's Wort flowers and leaves in a warm, dark place. This helps preserve their medicinal properties. Once dried, grind the herbs into a fine powder using a coffee grinder or mortar and pestle. Mix 10-15% of the St. John's Wort powder with a carrier oil like coconut or sweet almond oil in a small saucepan. Heat the mixture over low heat, stirring occasionally, until the oil is warm and the mixture is well combined. Remove from heat and let cool. Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean container to separate the oil from any remaining plant particles. Add a preservative like vitamin E oil to extend the shelf life of the cream. Finally, whip the mixture with a hand mixer or whisk until it becomes light and fluffy, similar to a lotion. Store the cream in an airtight container in the refrigerator, and apply it to the affected area as needed to help ease menstrual cramps.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hypericum perforatum herbal creams that help with menstrual cramps, start by gathering the necessary ingredients. You'll need dried Hypericum perforatum leaves and flowers, which can be sourced from a health food store or grown in your own garden. Next, combine the dried herbs with a carrier oil like coconut or olive oil in a small saucepan. Heat the mixture over low heat for about 10-15 minutes, stirring occasionally, to allow the herbs to infuse their properties into the oil. Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean glass jar, pressing on the solids to extract as much oil as possible. Then, mix the infused oil with a thickening agent like beeswax or shea butter to create a creamy texture. Heat the mixture again until the beeswax or shea butter has melted, then pour the cream into small jars and let it cool and solidify. This cream is made from the extract of the chaste tree, a plant that has been used for centuries to treat hormonal imbalances and menstrual issues. remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Hypericum perforatum herbal cream for menstrual cramps, you'll need dried St. John's Wort flowers and a few simple ingredients. First, steep 2 tablespoons of dried Hypericum perforatum flowers in 2 cups of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to make a tea. Let the tea cool down, then strain it to remove the flowers. Next, mix 1/2 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, and 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil in a double boiler. Add the cooled tea to the oil mixture and stir well. Once the mixture is smooth and combined, remove it from the heat and let it cool until it thickens. Finally, pour the cream into a container and store it in the refrigerator. When you need relief from menstrual cramps, simply apply a small amount of the cream to your lower abdomen and massage it in. The Hypericum perforatum in the cream has anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe and ease menstrual cramps, providing natural relief and comfort.

remedies.remedy_desc: Paeonia lactiflora creams are used to help with menstrual cramps because they contain a plant extract called peony. The peony root has been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries to ease pain and discomfort. It works by blocking the production of prostaglandins, which are chemicals in the body that cause the uterus to contract and lead to cramps. When prostaglandins are blocked, the uterus contracts less, reducing the pain and discomfort caused by menstrual cramps. This makes it easier for women to manage their symptoms and go about their daily activities without pain. The peony extract is also believed to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce swelling and discomfort in the lower abdomen.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Hypericum perforatum herbal cream to help with menstrual cramps, you'll need a few simple ingredients and some basic equipment. First, you'll need to dry some fresh St. John's Wort flowers and leaves, which are the parts of the plant that contain the active ingredients to help with pain relief. You can air-dry them by tying the flowers and leaves in small bunches and hanging them upside down in a warm, dry place. Once they're completely dry, you can grind them into a fine powder using a coffee grinder or a mortar and pestle. Next, you'll need to mix the powdered St. John's Wort with a carrier oil, such as coconut or olive oil, in a ratio of about 1 part St. John's Wort to 2 parts carrier oil. Heat the mixture gently in a double boiler or a saucepan set over a bowl of simmering water, until the oil has absorbed the herbs and the mixture has cooled. Finally, you'll need to add a thickening agent, such as beeswax, to the mixture and stir until it's fully incorporated. Pour the mixture into a small tin or jar and let it cool and set. Once it's set, you can use the cream as needed to help relieve menstrual cramps.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Hypericum perforatum herbal creams that help with menstrual cramps, you will need dried St. John's Wort flowers, a carrier oil, and a cream base. Start by infusing the dried flowers in the carrier oil, such as coconut oil or olive oil, by placing them in a clean glass jar and letting them sit in a cool, dark place for 2-3 weeks, shaking the jar every few days. intro_desc: Menstrual cramps can be a real problem for many women. They can be so painful that it's hard to move or focus on anything else. But there's good news - herbal creams can be a big help. These creams often contain herbs like ginger (Zingiber officinale) and black cohosh (Cimicifuga racemosa). Ginger is great because it has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce pain and swelling. Black cohosh is also helpful because it can ease muscle cramps and spasms.  When you have menstrual cramps, you might feel like you're stuck in bed, unable to do anything. This can be frustrating and affect your daily life. If you're missing work or school because of cramps, you're not only in pain, but you're also missing out on important activities and relationships. By using herbal creams, you can start to feel better and get back to living your life. You can exercise, spend time with friends, and focus on your work or school. This is important because it can help you feel more in control and confident. When you're not held back by pain, you can achieve more and feel better about yourself.
supplementary_best: A combination of 'Paeonia lactiflora', 'Valeriana officinalis', and 'Glycyrrhiza glabra' herbal creams can help relieve menstrual cramps. 'Paeonia lactiflora' is known for its anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce pain and swelling associated with menstrual cramps. 'Valeriana officinalis' is an herb that promotes relaxation and reduces muscle tension, helping to calm the uterus and alleviate cramps. 'Glycyrrhiza glabra', also known as licorice root, has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help ease cramp pain and soothe the digestive system, often affected during menstrual periods. This combination addresses the physical and emotional aspects of menstrual cramps, providing relief from pain and discomfort.
supplementary_avoid: When choosing herbal creams for menstrual cramps, it's essential to be cautious with certain ingredients. One such herb to avoid is 'Rauvolfia serpentina', also known as snake root. This herb contains compounds that can slow down the heart rate and lower blood pressure, which may worsen menstrual cramp symptoms if taken in excess. Another herb to be careful with is 'Aristolochia clematitis', also known as birthwort. This herb has been linked to kidney damage and other health issues when ingested, and applying it topically may cause skin irritation. Additionally, 'Boswellia serrata' should be used with caution, as high doses can cause stomach upset and interact with medications. 'Cassia auriculata' may cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals, and 'Commiphora mukul' may exacerbate stomach problems. Finally, 'Silybum marianum' (milk thistle) is not typically used for menstrual cramps, but it can cause allergic reactions in some people. If you have menstrual cramps, talk to your healthcare provider before using any herbal creams, and choose products with well-known, researched ingredients.
supplementary_other_remedies: Warming up with a hot bath or shower can help ease menstrual cramps. The heat increases blood flow to the uterus, relaxing the muscles and reducing discomfort. This is because heat therapy, also known as thermotherapy, helps to relax the smooth muscle of the uterus, which contracts to cause cramps. Additionally, applying a heating pad or hot compress to the lower abdomen can also provide relief.  Drinking warm liquids, such as tea or broth, can also help to soothe menstrual cramps. This is because warm liquids stimulate the body's natural pain-relieving chemicals, called endorphins, which are released in response to the heat. Furthermore, some research suggests that drinking warm liquids can also help to relax the uterus and reduce cramping.  Getting enough rest and practicing relaxation techniques, such as deep breathing or meditation, can also help to alleviate menstrual cramps. When we are stressed or anxious, our bodies produce more prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause the uterus to contract and lead to cramping. By relaxing and reducing stress, we can decrease the production of prostaglandins and reduce cramping.  Eating foods rich in omega-3 fatty acids, such as salmon or walnuts, can also help to ease menstrual cramps. Omega-3s have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce inflammation in the uterus and alleviate cramping. Additionally, some research suggests that omega-3s can also help to relax the smooth muscle of the uterus, reducing contractions and cramping.
supplementary_related_ailments: People who experience menstrual cramps often have other ailments related to their reproductive health. One common issue is endometriosis, a condition where tissue grows outside the uterus, causing pain, heavy bleeding, and fertility problems. The medicinal herb chamomile has anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe endometriosis symptoms. Its active compounds, apigenin and luteolin, can reduce inflammation and relax muscles, providing relief from pain and discomfort.  Another issue many women face is polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), a hormonal disorder that causes irregular periods, cysts on the ovaries, and weight gain. The medicinal herb dandelion root has been used to treat PCOS symptoms by regulating hormones and improving liver function. Its active compound, taraxasterol, can help reduce inflammation and improve insulin sensitivity, making it easier to manage weight and menstrual cycles.  Women with menstrual cramps are also more likely to experience migraines and headaches due to hormonal fluctuations. Feverfew, a medicinal herb, has been used to treat migraines by reducing inflammation and relaxing blood vessels. Its active compound, parthenolide, can prevent blood vessels from dilating, which can reduce migraine severity and frequency.  In addition, women with menstrual cramps may experience anxiety and depression due to the emotional burden of living with chronic pain. The medicinal herb passionflower has been used to treat anxiety and insomnia by promoting relaxation and reducing stress. Its active compound, flavonoids, can help regulate the nervous system and promote a sense of calm, making it easier to manage anxiety and depression.  Lastly, women with menstrual cramps may experience digestive issues, such as constipation or diarrhea, due to hormonal fluctuations. The medicinal herb peppermint has been used to treat digestive issues by relaxing the muscles in the digestive tract and improving digestion. Its active compound, menthol, can help reduce inflammation and improve gut motility, making it easier to manage digestive symptoms.  These medicinal herbs can provide relief from a range of symptoms related to menstrual cramps, but it's essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using any herbal remedies, especially if you're taking medication or have underlying health conditions.
